fact: March 16 sits exactly 5 days before the spring equinox, when day and night become perfectly equal twins!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is prime time for maple trees to start bleeding their sweet, sticky sap that becomes syrup on your pancakes.
Why do robins suddenly appear around this date? Because earthworms are finally wiggling closer to the surface as soil warms up - it's like a worm buffet!
March 16 marks the exact middle of the month when sunrise happens about 6 minutes earlier than it did just one week ago.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere places, this date falls smack in the middle of autumn, when leaves are turning brilliant orange and red.
Cats and dogs start shedding their thick winter coats around this time, leaving fuzzy tumbleweeds of fur rolling around your house.
This date falls in Pisces territory until March 20, making it one of the last chances to be born under the fish zodiac sign.
In northern regions, snow that falls on March 16 melts faster than a popsicle in summer because the sun's rays are getting seriously stronger.
Frogs in ponds start their loud, croaky love songs around this time of year, turning quiet marshes into amphibian concert halls.
March 16 is the 75th day of the year, which means you've already lived through more than 20% of the entire year!
In many places, this is when crocuses poke their purple and yellow heads through the last bits of snow, like nature's own surprise party.
The moon phase on March 16 changes every year, but when it's a full moon on this date, it's called the Worm Moon because earthworms start wiggling to the surface.
